title: Photon Efficiency of High-Dimensional Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: We investigate entanglement-based quantum key distribution protocols, with particular emphasis on their efficiency under realistic conditions of satellite quantum communications, where performance is limited by the low power of a received signal and background radiation. We focus on scenarios where each photon pair is used to encode multiple qubits in order to optimally utilize the weak signal. By optimizing over the source intensity and the number of encoded qubits we study the theoretical information limit for the QKD efficiency. We show that the optimal efficiency is attained for finite entangled photons pair production probability which is in contrast to conventional communication efficiency maximized in the limit of vanishing signal strength. The multiqubit encoding can enhance the secret key rate by up to an order of magnitude compared to single-qubit schemes.
summary: The study investigates how encoding multiple qubits into a single photon can maximize the secret key extracted per photon in satellite-based quantum communications. It finds that high-dimensional encoding significantly improves robustness against background noise and that optimal performance requires a finite, non-vanishing signal strength.
main_problem: Optimizing the photon efficiency of entanglement-based Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) in photon-starved regimes, such as satellite-to-ground communications.
main_result: High-dimensional encoding (multiple qubits per photon) can increase the secret key rate by up to an order of magnitude, and the optimal efficiency is achieved at a finite photon pair production probability.
method: Analytical derivation using the Lambert W function and numerical optimization of the secret key rate and Photon Key Efficiency (PKE) over source intensity and encoding order.
model_or_system: Entanglement-based QKD protocols (BBM92 and SARG04) using temporal-slot encoding in a satellite-to-ground/free-space channel characterized by high loss and background radiation.
key_observables: Photon Key Efficiency (PKE), Quantum Bit Error Rate (QBER), and secret key rate.
important_parameters: Number of encoded qubits (m), pair production probability (p_pair), noise-to-transmission ratio (n/eta), and channel visibility (V).
main_assumptions: Symmetric channels, low-noise regime (n/eta << 1), high-visibility measurements, and the use of the BBM92/SARG04 protocols.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the temporal-slot encoding mechanism; Figure 2 shows optimal PKE and pair production probability as functions of noise; Figure 3 compares PKE and QBER for dephasing and depolarizing channels; Figure 4 shows PKE scaling.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of photon efficiency in satellite QKD, describes the high-dimensional temporal encoding scheme, presents the mathematical framework for calculating key rates under noise, provides analytical approximations for the low-noise regime, and concludes with numerical results and scaling laws.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems researchers as it addresses the optimization of quantum communication protocols under realistic decoherence (dephasing and depolarization) and high-loss environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Encoding of multiple logical qubits into a single photon prepared as a superposition of temporal slots. Example is given for three qubits, m = 3. (a) The slot number written in the binary representation specifies the basis states of individual qubits. The presence of a photon in temporal slot with n = 3 corresponds to a product of qubit basis states |0⟩|1⟩|1⟩in computational basis. (b) The classical sequence of three bits can be equivalently encoded in 3 equatorial qubits states |ϕ2⟩|ϕ1⟩|ϕ0⟩by a light pulse in the time frame with the corresponding relative phase in each of the M slots. Transformation introduced by a cascade of interferometric stages (c). With a suitable choice of...

FIG. 2. Optimal PKE (a), the corresponding the pair-production probability (b) and (c) coding order, as functions of the decoherence parameters in Alice’s and Bob’s channels nA/ηA and nB/ηB for the asymmetric four-state BBM92 protocol and visibility parameter is V = 0.98.

FIG. 3. Optimal photon key efficiency (PKE) (a, d), the corresponding quantum bit error rate (QBER) (b, e), and the optimal pair-production probability (c, f) for different protocols. Panels (a–c) correspond to the dephasing channel model, while panels (d–f) correspond to the depolarizing channel model. The visibility parameter used for the calculation is V = 0.98. Thin black dashed lines show approximate analytical results.

FIG. 4. Photon key efficiency for asymmetric four-state BBM92 protocol with q = 1 as a function of pair production probability assuming dephasing channel with visibility V = 0.98. Numbers indicate the values of the noise parameter nb/η.
